How to Elope in Maryland: Legal Steps and Best Locations

How to legally elope in Maryland: marriage license requirements, the best outdoor locations, vendor checklist, and real cost data from a local planner.

J

John

Baltimore Wedding Planner

Featured image, replace with real photo

Maryland is one of the most elopement-friendly states on the East Coast. There's no residency requirement, no waiting period for a marriage license in most counties, permits for outdoor ceremonies are accessible and affordable, and the setting options range from Atlantic beach to mountain waterfall to Chesapeake Bay waterfront.

I've planned elopements across Maryland for couples who wanted everything from a 10-minute courthouse ceremony to a fully coordinated waterfront ceremony with 15 guests and a photographer. Here's exactly how to do it legally, where to hold it, what it'll cost, and what to watch out for.

What Does It Mean to Elope in Maryland?

In Maryland, eloping means getting legally married in an intimate ceremony, typically with no guests or a very small group (under 15). The legal requirements are identical to any other Maryland wedding: both of you must appear at a Maryland Circuit Court to apply for a marriage license, hold a ceremony with a registered officiant, and have the license signed and filed within 60 days.

What makes Maryland elopements particularly workable is the combination of no residency requirement, same-day license issuance in most counties, accessible park permits, and a genuinely diverse range of ceremony settings within a compact geographic area. You don't need to leave the state for a beautiful elopement, and you won't have to spend $30,000 to pull one off.

How Do You Legally Elope in Maryland? Step by Step

  1. Apply for your Maryland marriage license. Both of you must appear together at a Maryland Circuit Court with valid photo ID and your Social Security numbers. If either of you has been previously married, bring a certified copy of the divorce decree or death certificate. Fees run $65 to $85 depending on the county. Most counties issue the license the same day.
  2. Choose your ceremony location and get any required permits. Public parks in Maryland require permits for groups over 25. For elopements with fewer than 25 people, many Baltimore City parks (including Patterson Park) require no permit. State parks require a DNR permit ($50 to $200). County parks have their own systems. If you're planning a private venue, confirm event permissions with the property owner.
  3. Book a registered officiant. Maryland accepts judges, justices of the peace, licensed religious ministers, and online-ordained individuals. Universal Life Church ordinations are valid in Maryland. Your officiant must be listed on the marriage license and sign it at the ceremony.
  4. Arrange any vendors you want. Photographer, florist, and musician are optional but common for elopements with photos. One witness is legally required. Most couples bring a friend or have the photographer serve as witness.
  5. Hold your ceremony and have the officiant sign the license. Don't leave the ceremony location without the signed license in hand. It's the most common oversight I see, and it's genuinely preventable.
  6. File the signed license with the issuing Circuit Court within 60 days. The officiant can return it, or you can bring it in yourself. The marriage isn't officially on record until it's filed.

What Are the Best Places to Elope in Maryland?

Maryland's variety of settings is one of its strongest qualities for elopements. You've got beach, mountain, bay waterfront, historic city, and countryside all within a few hours of each other. Here are the locations I've used most often, with honest notes on what each one actually delivers.

Patterson Park (Baltimore City)

No permit for under 25 people. $50-$300 for larger groups.

The historic pagoda overlooking the park is one of the most recognizable ceremony settings in Baltimore. Free for elopements under 25. You'll bring your own chairs and equipment. Best for couples who want a city setting with genuine character. I've done elopements here early on weekend mornings before the park fills with visitors.

Quiet Waters Park (Annapolis)

Anne Arundel County permit: $50-$150 for ceremony

South River waterfront in the Annapolis area. Formal gardens for the ceremony, views of the river, year-round access. One of the best-balanced locations in Maryland for elopements: genuinely beautiful, accessible, affordable permit, and easy for guests coming from Baltimore or DC. I recommend this one most often for Annapolis-area elopements.

Cunningham Falls State Park (Frederick County)

Maryland DNR permit: $100-$200

Maryland's largest waterfall in the Catoctin Mountains. The setting is genuinely dramatic. Getting to the falls requires a short hike, so this works best for couples with mobile guests and a willingness to coordinate the logistics. Best for fall foliage season and couples who want mountain settings over waterfront.

Assateague Island National Seashore

National Park Service permit: $150

Atlantic Ocean beach on Maryland's barrier island, known for its wild ponies. The beach setting creates ceremony photos that look nothing like a typical Maryland wedding. The permit process runs through the National Park Service and is simple and accessible for groups under 25. Best for couples who specifically want a beach elopement.

Annapolis City Dock Area

No permit for groups under 10 in public waterfront areas

The public waterfront around City Dock and the Eastport waterfront creates a sailboat-harbor backdrop for very small elopements. No permit required for under 10 people. Best for true elopements of 2 to 5 people where the setting and simplicity are the priorities.

Tilghman Island (Eastern Shore)

Private venue access required

Chesapeake Bay waterfront on a small island with a genuine destination feel. Best for elopements where the "feels like we left Maryland" experience matters to the couple. Requires coordinating private venue access or inn booking, but the setting genuinely delivers that quality.

How Much Does It Cost to Elope in Maryland?

Total Maryland elopement costs range from $100 (courthouse ceremony only) to $3,500+ for a fully coordinated outdoor ceremony with a photographer. Here's how it breaks down so you can see exactly what you're paying for at each level.

Elopement type Total cost range What's included
Courthouse ceremony only$100 to $250License ($75) + courthouse fee ($25 to $75) + optional gift for friend officiant
Park elopement, no photographer$200 to $600License + park permit ($50-$200) + officiant ($150-$400)
Park elopement with photographer$1,500 to $2,500License + permit + officiant + photographer ($1,000-$1,800 for 2 hours)
Full elopement with planner$1,800 to $3,500All above + elopement planner ($300-$750) + florals ($150-$400)

What Do You Need to Bring to a Maryland Elopement?

  • Signed marriage license (obtained at Circuit Court before the ceremony)
  • Registered officiant (confirmed and listed on the license before the ceremony)
  • At least one witness (a friend, the photographer, or the planner)
  • Park permit (if applicable for your location)
  • Any ceremony equipment you're using (arch, flowers, chairs for seated guests)
  • Plan for the signed license after the ceremony (don't leave without it)

Having John coordinate your Maryland elopement

John handles the permit applications, officiant booking, vendor coordination, and day-of logistics. He's done this at Quiet Waters Park, Patterson Park, Cunningham Falls, Assateague Island, and locations across the Eastern Shore. Elopement coordination packages start at $300.

See Elopement Packages

Frequently Asked Questions About Eloping in Maryland

Can you elope in Maryland the same day you get the license?

Yes. Most Maryland counties (including Baltimore City, Anne Arundel, Howard, Frederick, and Harford) issue marriage licenses immediately after you apply. There's no mandatory waiting period. You can apply in the morning and elope in the afternoon. Montgomery and Prince George's counties have historically required a 48-hour wait, so apply there two days before the ceremony. See the full county-by-county guide.

Do you need witnesses to elope in Maryland?

Yes. Maryland law requires one witness to be present at the ceremony and sign the marriage license. Most elopements have the photographer or planner serve as the witness, or one guest if you're bringing a small group. The witness signs the license at the ceremony alongside the officiant.

Is it illegal to elope without telling family?

No. Eloping without family present or without informing family is completely legal. The legal requirements are just the marriage license, a registered officiant, and one witness. What you tell your family and when is entirely your decision. Many couples elope first and hold a celebration party for family later.

How do you find a good elopement photographer in Maryland?

Look for photographers who list elopements or intimate weddings in their portfolio specifically. Elopement photography requires different skills than large wedding photography: working with 2 to 6 people, managing outdoor lighting in park and waterfront settings, and creating meaningful photos without the large wedding party structure. Many excellent wedding photographers aren't the right fit for elopements, and vice versa. Ask to see full elopement galleries, not just highlight reels.

For more on the legal process, see how to get married in Maryland and the Maryland marriage license guide.
